source: Rocky Linux Admin Guide, ch. 5.1 uniq command (pp. 71-72)

questions.q: Why must the input to uniq be sorted first?
options: uniq only compares consecutive (adjacent) lines, so duplicates must be brought together first
uniq cannot read an unsorted file at all
sort is what actually removes the duplicates; uniq only counts
It is not necessary; uniq sorts internally
questions.why: uniq compares only lines that follow each other, so scattered duplicates are missed unless sort groups them first.

questions.q: Sort firstnames.txt and collapse runs of identical lines into one (the plain, no-option case).
questions.why: With no option, uniq drops consecutive duplicate lines, leaving one of each distinct name.

questions.q: From firstnames.txt, show only the lines that appear EXACTLY ONCE.
questions.why: -u prints only the lines that are unique (occur a single time).

questions.q: From firstnames.txt, show only the lines that appear AT LEAST TWICE (one line per such name).
questions.why: -d prints one copy of each line that is duplicated (appears two or more times).

questions.q: What is the difference between 'uniq -d' and 'uniq -D'?
options: -d prints one copy of each duplicated line; -D prints ALL copies of every duplicated line
-d prints all copies; -D prints one copy
questions.why: -d gives a single representative of each duplicated line; -D emits every occurrence of the lines that are duplicated.

questions.q: Count how many times each line occurs in firstnames.txt (prefix each line with its count).
questions.why: -c prefixes each output line with the number of times it occurred - the classic 'top offenders' idiom for log analysis.
